topics.topic_name: How much water do we use

cards.question: About three-fourth of the Earth’s surface is covered with water . That is why it is also called the water planet.
cards.text_html: About three-fourth of the Earth’s surface is covered with water. That is why it is also called the water planet.

cards.question: Ocean i s the largest source of water. Water in oceans and seas has large amount of salt so it is not fit for drinking.
cards.text_html: Ocean is the largest source of water. Water in oceans and seas has large amount of salt so it is not fit for drinking.

cards.question: Only about 0.003% of the fresh water i s easily available to us in the form of groundwater, river, lake, stream, soil moisture, and water vapour.
cards.text_html: Only about 0.003% of the fresh water is easily available to us in the form of groundwater, river, lake, stream, soil moisture, and water vapour.

topics.topic_name: Where do we get water from?

cards.question: Water exists in all the three states – snow as solid, water as liquid and water vapour as gas . Snow is the solid state of water, tap water is the liquid state of water and water vapour in air is the water in gas state.
cards.text_html: Water exists in all the three states – snow as solid, water as liquid and water vapour as gas. Snow is the solid state of water, tap water is the liquid state of water and water vapour in air is the water in gas state.

cards.question: In India, about 70% of the total water available is used for agriculture , 20-22% by industrie s and only 8% i s used for p ersonal or domestic needs.
cards.text_html: In India, about 70% of the total water available is used for agriculture, 20-22% by industries and only 8% is used for personal or domestic needs.

cards.question: Agricultural needs: Our country depends a lot on agriculture. Farmers rely on water to sustain their agricultural crops, e.g., wheat, paddy, etc.
cards.text_html: Agricultural needs: Our country depends a lot on agriculture. Farmers rely on water to sustain their agricultural crops, e.g., wheat, paddy, etc.

cards.question: When wet clothes are spread on a rope, they dry up because the water evaporates from them. This process of changing water into water vapour is called evaporation.
cards.text_html: When wet clothes are spread on a rope, they dry up because the water evaporates from them. This process of changing water into water vapour is called evaporation.

cards.question: When water having salt dissolved in it is heated on a burner, water evaporates rapidly and goes into the air, but the salt is left behind. When ocean water gets heated by the heat of the sun, the water evaporates to form water vapour which goes into the air, and salt is left behind.
cards.text_html: When water having salt dissolved in it is heated on a burner, water evaporates rapidly and goes into the air, but the salt is left behind. When ocean water gets heated by the heat of the sun, the water evaporates to form water vapour which goes into the air, and salt is left behind.

cards.question: Water droplets in the clouds keep bumping against one another and sometimes stick to form bigger drops. When these drops become too heavy to float in the air, they drop down back to the Earth as rain. The water that comes down as rain, in time, evaporates and goes up to form clouds again. This leads to forming a cycle, known as the water cycle.
cards.text_html: Water droplets in the clouds keep bumping against one another and sometimes stick to form bigger drops. When these drops become too heavy to float in the air, they drop down back to the Earth as rain. The water that comes down as rain, in time, evaporates and goes up to form clouds again. This leads to forming a cycle, known as the water cycle.

cards.question: The water cycle is the cyclic movement of water from the atmosphere to the Earth and back to the atmosphere through various processes.
cards.text_html: The water cycle is the cyclic movement of water from the atmosphere to the Earth and back to the atmosphere through various processes.

cards.question: Loss of water by Plants : Plants need water to grow which they get from the soil. They use a part of water to make their food and release the remaining water into the air as water vapour through small pores in their leaves, called stomata. This process is called transpiration.
cards.text_html: Loss of water by Plants: Plants need water to grow which they get from the soil. They use a part of water to make their food and release the remaining water into the air as water vapour through small pores in their leaves, called stomata. This process is called transpiration.

cards.question: Clouds are formed by a process of condensation . Condensation is the reverse of evaporation.
cards.text_html: Clouds are formed by a process of condensation. Condensation is the reverse of evaporation.

cards.question: The tiny drops of dew on the leaves of the grass are seen on the cold winter morning. The formation of dew is due to condensation of water vapour present in air.
cards.text_html: The tiny drops of dew on the leaves of the grass are seen on the cold winter morning. The formation of dew is due to condensation of water vapour present in air.

cards.question: Evaporatio n: Sun’s heat changes the water in oceans, rivers, ponds, and other water bodies into water vapour. Transpiration Plants give off water vapour through their leaves.
cards.text_html: Evaporation: Sun’s heat changes the water in oceans, rivers, ponds, and other water bodies into water vapour. Transpiration Plants give off water vapour through their leaves.

cards.question: The water which was initially taken away from rivers, lakes, ponds, oceans and soil, etc. by evaporation and transpiration is finally put back into them. This happens over and over again leading to water cycle.
cards.text_html: The water which was initially taken away from rivers, lakes, ponds, oceans and soil, etc. by evaporation and transpiration is finally put back into them. This happens over and over again leading to water cycle.

cards.question: Water cycle is the continuous circulation of water from the earth’s surface to the atmosphere, and from the atmosphere back to earth, is called the water cycle.
cards.text_html: Water cycle is the continuous circulation of water from the earth’s surface to the atmosphere, and from the atmosphere back to earth, is called the water cycle.

cards.question: Importance of water cycle: The water cycle makes fresh water available in the form of rain. The water cycle keeps the amount of water on the earth’s surface constant.
cards.text_html:
Importance of water cycle:

  1. The water cycle makes fresh water available in the form of rain.
  2. The water cycle keeps the amount of water on the earth’s surface constant.

cards.question: Water cycle can be defined as the process through which water gets evaporated from open surfaces like oceans and seas, gets condensed as it rises in the cool atmosphere, and ultimately pours down as rain (precipitation) back into oceans, lakes, rivers, and ponds.
cards.text_html: Water cycle can be defined as the process through which water gets evaporated from open surfaces like oceans and seas, gets condensed as it rises in the cool atmosphere, and ultimately pours down as rain (precipitation) back into oceans, lakes, rivers, and ponds.

cards.question: Advantages of Rains Rains bring relief by cooling the environment after hot summer days. The sowing of many crops depends on the arrival of rains during monsoon. Rains provide water in the rivers and dams of hydroelectric power plants. Rains fill the lakes and ponds which act as a source of water. Rains are responsible for the groundwater which gets stored under the surface of the earth.
cards.text_html:
Advantages of Rains

  • Rains bring relief by cooling the environment after hot summer days.
  • The sowing of many crops depends on the arrival of rains during monsoon.
  • Rains provide water in the rivers and dams of hydroelectric power plants.
  • Rains fill the lakes and ponds which act as a source of water.
  • Rains are responsible for the groundwater which gets stored under the surface of the earth.

cards.question: The excessive rainfall due to heavy rains leads to many problems. Some of them are: Heavy rains in the city lead to waterlogging of roads and streets which causes inconvenience to the people. Heavy rains may cause floods as the river water starts overflowing from its banks and spread over a large area of land. The animals living in the water are carried away by the fast-flowing flood waters. Heavy rains also kill the animals living in the soil as they do not get air to breathe when the soil gets covered with flood water.
cards.text_html:
The excessive rainfall due to heavy rains leads to many problems. Some of them are:

  • Heavy rains in the city lead to waterlogging of roads and streets which causes inconvenience to the people.
  • Heavy rains may cause floods as the river water starts overflowing from its banks and spread over a large area of land.
  • The animals living in the water are carried away by the fast-flowing flood waters.
  • Heavy rains also kill the animals living in the soil as they do not get air to breathe when the soil gets covered with flood water.

cards.question: Just as too little rain is bad, too much rain is bad as well. It leads to water being everywhere, sometimes the entire area remains underwater or submerged. A condition when the ground becomes submerged under water, due to heavy rain and overflowing of rivers is called flood.
cards.text_html: Just as too little rain is bad, too much rain is bad as well. It leads to water being everywhere, sometimes the entire area remains underwater or submerged. A condition when the ground becomes submerged under water, due to heavy rain and overflowing of rivers is called flood.

cards.question: A flood can thus lead to disease, starvation, loss of life, and property. A flood also can have the same effect on the food chain as a drought does.
cards.text_html: A flood can thus lead to disease, starvation, loss of life, and property. A flood also can have the same effect on the food chain as a drought does.

topics.topic_name: What Happens If It Does Not Rain For A Long Period?

cards.question: Sometimes it does not rain in a region for a year or more. This leads to the shortage of water in that region causing dryness. This environment of dryness is called drought.
cards.text_html: Sometimes it does not rain in a region for a year or more. This leads to the shortage of water in that region causing dryness. This environment of dryness is called drought.

cards.question: The people living in areas having drought conditions face many difficulties. Some of the problems faced by people are: In drought conditions, the soil does not have sufficient moisture to grow crops leading to a shortage of food for people. In drought conditions, the vegetation such as grass and other fodder does not grow well leading to a shortage of fodder for domestic animals. Due to a shortage of food and fodder, many people living in drought areas migrate to other places along with their domestic animals in search of food and fodder. Some people and domestic animals may die of starvation.
cards.text_html:
The people living in areas having drought conditions face many difficulties. Some of the problems faced by people are:

  • In drought conditions, the soil does not have sufficient moisture to grow crops leading to a shortage of food for people.
  • In drought conditions, the vegetation such as grass and other fodder does not grow well leading to a shortage of fodder for domestic animals.
  • Due to a shortage of food and fodder, many people living in drought areas migrate to other places along with their domestic animals in search of food and fodder. Some people and domestic animals may die of starvation.

cards.question: To conserve water means 'to save water by using it carefully, wisely and by preventing its wastage' .
cards.text_html: To conserve water means 'to save water by using it carefully, wisely and by preventing its wastage'.

cards.question: Conservation of water can be done by building dams, avoiding wastage of water at homes, both indoors and outdoors, harvesting rainwater, and preventing pollution of water.
cards.text_html: Conservation of water can be done by building dams, avoiding wastage of water at homes, both indoors and outdoors, harvesting rainwater, and preventing pollution of water.

cards.question: Ways to conserve water are as follows: Turn off the tap immediately after use. Get the leaking taps repaired immediately. Take bath by filling water in a bucket and not directly under the flowing tap. Wash the utensils by filling the water in a basin and not under the running water. Use the water from washing rice, vegetables, or fruits for watering the plants at home. Do not use a full flush from the cistern in the toilet when a half flush is sufficient.
cards.text_html:
Ways to conserve water are as follows:

  • Turn off the tap immediately after use. Get the leaking taps repaired immediately.
  • Take bath by filling water in a bucket and not directly under the flowing tap.
  • Wash the utensils by filling the water in a basin and not under the running water.
  • Use the water from washing rice, vegetables, or fruits for watering the plants at home.
  • Do not use a full flush from the cistern in the toilet when a half flush is sufficient.

cards.question: Building Dam: A dam is a structure built to hold back water in order to prevent floods, and to provide water for irrigation and storage. Building a dam is a solution to both drought and flood.
cards.text_html: Building Dam: A dam is a structure built to hold back water in order to prevent floods, and to provide water for irrigation and storage. Building a dam is a solution to both drought and flood.

cards.question: Rainwater harvesting: The process of collecting and storing rainwater from roofs or a surface catchment is called rainwater harvesting.
cards.text_html: Rainwater harvesting: The process of collecting and storing rainwater from roofs or a surface catchment is called rainwater harvesting.

cards.question: Rainwater Harvesting: One way of increasing the availability of water to overcome its shortage is rainwater harvesting. There are two main techniques for rainwater harvesting: Collection and storage of rainwater in tanks for future use when there is the scarcity of water. Percolation of rainwater into the ground by constructing percolation pits to recharge groundwater.
cards.text_html:
Rainwater Harvesting: One way of increasing the availability of water to overcome its shortage is rainwater harvesting. There are two main techniques for rainwater harvesting:

  1. Collection and storage of rainwater in tanks for future use when there is the scarcity of water.
  2. Percolation of rainwater into the ground by constructing percolation pits to recharge groundwater.

cards.question: Rainwater harvesting can be done in two ways: Rooftop rainwater harvesting : In this method, the rainwater is collected from the rooftop to a storage tank through pipes. This water may contain soil particles from the roof and needs to be filtered before use. Instead of collecting rainwater in the tank, the pipes can go directly into the percolation pit in the ground. The water seeps into the soil to recharge or refill the groundwater. Rainwater harvesting from open spaces around buildings. Rainwater goes into the ground directly from the roadside.
cards.text_html:
Rainwater harvesting can be done in two ways:

  1. Rooftop rainwater harvesting: In this method, the rainwater is collected from the rooftop to a storage tank through pipes. This water may contain soil particles from the roof and needs to be filtered before use. Instead of collecting rainwater in the tank, the pipes can go directly into the percolation pit in the ground. The water seeps into the soil to recharge or refill the groundwater.
  2. Rainwater harvesting from open spaces around buildings. Rainwater goes into the ground directly from the roadside.
